diagrams.description: Political Communication studies the exchange of messages between political actors, media, and the public. This flowchart outlines the process: the ultimate goal is to understand and explain how communication shapes political power and public opinion. It integrates core components: political actors (candidates, parties, governments) crafting messages and campaigns; media systems and channels (traditional mass media, digital platforms, interpersonal networks) that filter and transmit them; and audiences (citizens, voters, interest groups) who receive, interpret, and respond. These components are analyzed through major theoretical frameworks—agenda‑setting, framing, priming, spiral of silence, uses and gratifications, and cultivation theory—that explain influence pathways. The political communication cycle (actors encode → media gatekeep → audiences decode → feedback) continuously shapes outcomes such as political participation, polarization, legitimation, and policy change.
